from Youtube:

Ronnie mentioned in the interview that someone should check the stats... well here we go.

Ronnie has 28 seasons as a professional
Hendry has 31 seasons as a professional

Ronnie has played 1311 matches winning 75,3% (highest winning percentage in the sport)
Hendry has played 1270 matches winning, 68,9%

Ronnie has made his 1054 century breaks in 11,452 frames (a ratio of 10.87). Currently, only Judd Trump has a slightly better century break ratio with 10.62, but it fair to say that Trump is young and on fire... chances are his ratio will degrade when he's past his 40th birthday. Ronnie also won 61.25% of those frames (7014)

Hendry has made his 772 century breaks in 11,722 frames (a ratio of 15.18)... and even though he has played more frames than Ronnie, he has won less frames than him (6891)

They have played 56 matches against each other, Ronnie has won 30 of them against 21 wins for Hendry and 5 draws. They have also met 13 times in tournament finals with 9 wins for Ronnie.

Let's talk tournaments... Ronnie said that it took him more tournaments to beat Hendry's records but as we see here, that's impossible because with a better Win/Loss ration and nearly the same amount of matches played, there is no way that Ronnie has played on more tournaments than Hendry. Also, every snooker fan who follows the sport, is well aware that for the past 20 years Ronnie is intentionally missing a lot of tournaments. God knows how much titles, wins and centuries he could have earned if he was a more hungry player, like Trump for example. Just to compare, Judd Trump is now 30 years old and got into top 16 for the first time in 2011 for such a short period he already has played 1111 matches (with win percentage of 69,67), only 200 matches less than Ronnie, who has been on the tour from 1993. That's because Trump is hungry and determined to set records... which is why in 8-10 years he will probably surpass Ronnie in centuries, but not because he's a better break builder, but because he plays everywhere he can. They have very close century break ratios, but in the statistics of 90+, 80+ and 70+ breaks Ronnies has much, much better ratio than Trump... which for me solidifies him as the better break builder. But let's get back to Hendry....

Hendry also played more finals to get his 36 ranking titles and his 39 non ranking titles, so Ronnie has a better win/loss ratio in finals where the pressure is at it's high. Not to mention that Ronnie won the majority of his titles in the same era that Hendry could not survive, retiring at the age of 42 with the last ranking title win at the age of 35 and last world title win at the age of 30

Is there any doubt who's the greatest player?

They have played 56 matches against each other, Ronnie has won 30 of them against 21 wins for Hendry and 5 draws. They have also met 13 times in tournament finals with 9 wins for Ronnie.

Let's talk tournaments... Ronnie said that it took him more tournaments to beat Hendry's records but as we see here, that's impossible because with a better Win/Loss ration and nearly the same amount of matches played, there is no way that Ronnie has played on more tournaments than Hendry. Also, every snooker fan who follows the sport, is well aware that for the past 20 years Ronnie is intentionally missing a lot of tournaments. God knows how much titles, wins and centuries he could have earned if he was a more hungry player, like Trump for example. Just to compare, Judd Trump is now 30 years old and got into top 16 for the first time in 2011 for such a short period he already has played 1111 matches (with win percentage of 69,67), only 200 matches less than Ronnie, who has been on the tour from 1993. That's because Trump is hungry and determined to set records... which is why in 8-10 years he will probably surpass Ronnie in centuries, but not because he's a better break builder, but because he plays everywhere he can. They have very close century break ratios, but in the statistics of 90+, 80+ and 70+ breaks Ronnies has much, much better ratio than Trump... which for me solidifies him as the better break builder. But let's get back to Hendry....

Hendry also played more finals to get his 36 ranking titles and his 39 non ranking titles, so Ronnie has a better win/loss ratio in finals where the pressure is at it's high. Not to mention that Ronnie won the majority of his titles in the same era that Hendry could not survive, retiring at the age of 42 with the last ranking title win at the age of 35 and last world title win at the age of 30

Is there any doubt who's the greatest player?
